speaker: interviewer
text: The date is Monday, December the 4, 1995. The survivor is Henia Goldman. Her maiden name is Meller. The interviewer is Sharon Tyler. We're in Sutton, Surrey, England, and the language of the interview is English. I'm Sharon Tyler. It's Monday the 4th of December, 1995. I'm interviewing Henia Goldman, and we're in Sutton, Surrey, England. Please tell me your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Henia Goldman. H-E-N-I-A G-O-L-D-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Meller. M-E-L-L-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell me your date of birth and age now.

speaker: interviewee
text: 9th of August, 1926. So I would be 70 next year, 69 now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city and country of your birth, and if you could spell the name of your city.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was a small town called Sadowa Wisznia. S-A-D-O-W-I-S-Z-N-I-A, Sadowa Wisznia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Can you tell me something about what you remember of your childhood in Sadowa Wisznia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was a happy childhood. We had an extended family. My mother was one of a large family. My father was one of a large family. There were many aunts and aunts and cousins. We were considered well off. Relatively, you can't measure up, but relative to the community. It was a small community. We lived-- Sadowa Wisznia you would call is a shtetl. Shtetl is a small town. And it had all the character of a small town. Very closely knit. Community was very caring. We didn't have committees, but we had families who took upon themselves to look after the people who were poor or deprived. There's a lot of poverty. I had a wonderful grandmother, who was dedicated to-- to charity work. I was her secretary. So every Friday, we used to distribute orders. She used to have a grocery order. I was embarrassed, because some of the houses were children that I knew in school. But I was her secretary, and I would deliver it. Sometimes it was frosty and cold, but I did it. She was an example to us all. Very religious background, very orthodox. My father was very, very orthodox, my grandmother, my mother, too. I had a conflict with my father, because he was against education, because he thought that education will have a level of reducing my commitment to Judaism, you know. But I began to appreciate his attitude during the war, because I realized that his beliefs were really true. It wasn't superficial. He was really religious, and he didn't want to modernize. He wanted to keep up the religious attitudes. As he didn't have sons, he concentrated on us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I used to go to a Jew school, which was called Bais Yaakov. This was after ordinary school. We used to go to school from 8:00 to 1:00, and then we had the Jewish Bais Yaakov from 2:00 to 5:00 every day.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the one who originated, because he was very appalled that his daughter didn't speak Yiddish, because we spoke already Polish at home. And he realized that education is important. So he went to Krakow, which was the center of Bais Yaakov, and he brought the teacher, who lived originally with us, but later when it was organized, in a school form and he was to attend it. I had two younger sisters. I was the older one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So life was relatively comfortable. It was very warm. The environment was not very nice, because Poland was very anti-Semitic, and therefore the family warmth was to-- to put us-- to make us comfortable, to comfort us against-- I remember my father was-- my grandfather was bitten by a dog, you know, because as a Jew. During the sort of Catholic holidays, we would be afraid to go on the street, because there was quite a lot of violence too. So outside, the world was tough, and anti-Semitism was there on every step. But inside the family was very warm and comforting and very close. When you said you lived in a shtetl, were you in a-- mainly amongst Jewish people in the area of the town where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was thinking about the composition of the population. It's difficult, but I would say that perhaps 40% of the population was Jewish in the little town, 40% Ukrainian, and 20% Poles, because it was part of the Ukraine. And-- and we lived only with Jews. We only mixed with Jews. I didn't have any contact with-- except in class, in school. But, we-- we-- the whole center of the town, the market with the building around the market, was mostly Jewish shops and Jewish people. And we had about three synagogues in this little shtetl. It was a very Jewish background.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your-- you said your father was extremely religious--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very orthodox. Hasidic. He belonged to the [NON-ENGLISH] rabbi, and he was a real believer. He was-- he was afraid-- I missed the word. What I wanted to say is assimilation. He was against it, and he thought that education would bring assimilation. And also because we didn't have secondary education, it meant going to Lwow to gymnasium. And the family in Lwow was already more-- less religious, so he was afraid of this influence. And I had '38, a very sad year, because I wanted to go to further education, which he objected to it. But I know now that he meant it, because he was really a very, very true, even-- he was tested. You know, very often during the war, I could see he would rather not eat, however hungry he was, if it wasn't kosher. If he went to work, he would come back on the Sabbath and dress in black and study the Tanakh. Also, it was forbidden he would communicate with other people. So we had contact over the roofs. He was really a very true, religious man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did that affect-- were you-- presumably, as a child, you were equally, you were part of that and you led--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: --A very religious life.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a very religious-- I never questioned it. This was that. I lost my God on the way, so now I haven't got it. But I am Jewish by culture, but religiously I've lost my God on the way. Because, especially as my grandmother was in the last stages on the train with me, and I ask her, why are you here? How is it? Because she was really an extraordinary person. And she had the true religion, because she told me, God wishes like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So to the last minute, she really believed. They were very religious people, good people. Religious people on the whole. Perhaps the young people were already influenced by new ideas, left-wing intellectual ideas. But most of the people that I'm extended, we mixed in, were very orthodox and religious. Do you think that there are particular things that your family did as a Hasidic family, that-- I mean, are there things you can tell me that were specific and interesting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there are many things that I remember. I mean, we always, when we had a simcha celebration, it was always communal. It was, again, shared with other people. It was never just a simcha for ourselves. I told you that the weekly parcel. Any Jewish with the-- when there was a Jewish holiday, the parcels would include for Pesach, matzohs and wine. For Purim, other things. It was a very-- she really dedicated her life. She was very religious. She was praying three times a day. But there was no committee, so it depended on two or three richer families, relatively, who took upon themselves to look after the poor. At one stage, she was going to have a new coat, a fur coat. But there was an orphan who had to get married, and she didn't hesitate to say, I don't need the coat. She needs it. It was something that it was natural, you know, it was part of a way of life. One didn't have praises for it. It was an accepted form--

speaker: interviewer
text: I met, in Israel, somebody who told me the story about my family. I find it very remarkable. When my mother was born, there was another child born at that place, whose mother was very ill, and we couldn't find feeding, you know, milk, what is it called, the nurse who feeds the babies? Midwife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Midwife, something, who would do it. So my grandmother would not tell anybody in the family, and go and feed this child. Now, the standard of hygiene was very primitive at that time. And this was a very infectious disease, I don't remember. So she would do this, before feeding her own child, go to feed this child. This child lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a very sharing--

speaker: interviewee
text: High standard to follow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, a very high standard to follow. So in the-- I understand it was a very sharing and caring community that you were in. Your grandmother, you say, prayed three times a day. How were women religiously? I mean, was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, because education for women was neglected, you know. The boys used to go to Cheder, but education for girls was very limited. And this is why my father realized that something has to be done.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sometimes I think, do I glamorize, you know, when you lose something, when you don't have it? Do I exaggerate? Do I 'examorate?' Maybe there is something. Maybe I just remember the very nice things that happened. But it has good memories, fond memories for you. What was it like being the oldest of three-- of three girls. How was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, during-- I mean, at the later stage-- I know you wanted to talk rather before the war. During the war, I took the responsibility to maintain the whole family, because my father was taken away. My mother was a very gentle soul. She just couldn't fight. She just was not-- she-- she didn't have the spirit or the strength to-- to-- to fight. So I was responsible for the maintenance of the whole family at a very early age.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there's a lot of responsibility on you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But on the whole, talking about pre-wartime, the childhood. You know, the difference between us, my sister was about four years younger, and the other one was also about three or four years. So there was a space between us. I don't remember when I fought with them, but I just remember the nice things we did together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you remember getting on fairly well with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You presumably helped your mother a lot in the home as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not so much. Funny enough, my father criticized my mother. How are you bringing up your daughter? She won't be able to be a housewife later on. Because I was interested in reading and studying and learning, my mother always said, when she need it, she'll learn.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she used to always give me the opportunities or the freedom to do my own thing and to study. Don't forget that we always had help in the house too, you know, so it-- the atmosphere wasn't like today. We didn't have already made things or tinned stuff or micro ovens, so cooking was starting from 9:00 until lunchtime, with the help.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she didn't require my help, and she also facilitated-- gave me the choice and the time to do the things, because I was always very curious, and I was quite bright in school. And went from 8:00 to 1:00 and then from 2:00 to 5:00. You know, there was very little time for homework or for reading. So I had to-- I didn't have to help. I didn't even know how water boils. I always remember telling the children an incident, and when-- Because I was my grandmother's favorite, in the summer, she had a summer house we used to go to. And she had to have injection. The doctor came and talked to her, and he said would you watch it, and when the water boils, put the needle in, because at that time they didn't have separate needles. And I was ashamed to admit it, I didn't know how water boils. I was so ignorant. But I've learned the hard way.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure you have. I'm sure you have. How did you come to be your grandmother's favorite?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because locally, you know, the grandchildren lived in different towns. Not all in the one Sadowa Wisznia and from this being the oldest and so she-- I had a close link with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, how would you-- You said that you enjoyed reading and were studying. How would you describe yourself at that time? What sort of-- what kind of child were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was very bright in class. I was always top of the class, I was always, you know-- It sounds like bragging, but it was a fact. And, this was elementary school. We didn't have gymnasium, which is the grammar school here. So school started from 8 to 13. And, then, so this was elementary class. And the classes were overcrowded. The school was one school in town. So they had two shifts from 8:00 to 1:00 and the afternoon was another shift. Because the classes were mixed. But I was always very curious, and I think curiosity was one of the factors which contributed to my survival. Also, in England, I know curiosity is a non you characteristic. I'm all for curiosity, because it-- it was the spear-head, and you know, the sort of instinct to find out what life is all about, which probably helped or was one the factors in my survival.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to Hebrew school in the afternoons.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yiddish. Yiddish school.

speaker: interviewee
text: We did have Tanakh. We had, you know, we learned Hebrew and modern Hebrew, and we used to, you know, do Tanakh and Yiddish grammar, Yiddish poetry, writing. It was mostly Yiddish-orientated. There was a Zionist organization there, too. I was almost too young and somehow I didn't join the Zionist movement. Also there was a Zionist group in Sadowa Wisznia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so the teaching in the afternoon was not so much a religious teaching, but a more, a language--

speaker: interviewee
text: And religious. [INTERPOSING VOICES] We did Jewish history and Tanakh, you know. And we used to do the Torah. You know, we used to have quite a lot of lessons from that. It was both, you know, both literary and religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's business, what was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he had a grocery business. We had a sort of-- apparently the house is still there. I've never gone back to Poland. Maybe I'll have some courage and go back to the place. But it is sort of the main building in the square. And my grandfather's business and my aunt's business and our father's business were next to each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they? What were your grandfather's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my grandfather had a wholesale in alcohol and beer. My aunt had a wholesale business in tobacco and cigarettes, which was quite a lot. And we had a grocery business. So I knew all three. I used to help out when the necessary was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was for the-- for the Jewish community, the shop, or-- or for--

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean, for Jewish community?

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, was it-- did it serve-- did you have people coming in who were from the non-Jewish parts of town? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Of course, I mean, most of the population, the shops were open. We had a market day, which the people from villages used to come, and they used to barter or exchange-- not barter, but exchange. They used to bring their products from the-- from the villages and then buy things in the shop. So the shops were open to everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you deal with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jewish people?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it kosher or non-kosher things, or both?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were only kosher. I mean, we didn't have meat. The butchers were separate. But most of the Jewish people would have kosher. Butchers, there was non-existent. The first time I had a piece of ham, I had to vomit, I was so sick. There was a sort of psychological almost breakthrough to have the first time non-Jewish, non-kosher food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, most of the-- so all of the Jewish community was equally orthodox to your-- to your family, or less?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, perhaps less and more. I mean, there were already sort of assimilated and intellectual people who had university education, like lawyers and doctors. So there were-- there was a group. We didn't mix with them. You know, there were-- We knew about them. We mixed, but our circle of people were mostly very religious and orthodox. But there were people already. There was a lot of-- if I could sort of summarize it, or when I look back is most people think that small town, you know, there is a characteristics mentality. Yet I found something very, very special about the small town. Most people here, when you talk to them, they come either from Warsaw or from-- nobody comes from a small town. I always emphasize I come from a small town, from a shtetl. Because there was this-- because you were surrounded with very unpleasant and negative attitude, because there was a lot of poverty, because you were limited, you didn't have access to-- to-- to further education. You didn't have gymnasium. There was a small library, but even that, very small. You had enormous curiosity for things from outside. You had reading and discussion and talk and magazines. You know, somebody would have one and bring it around. It was a big interest going on, what is happening in the world outside. Because our life and our town or our horizon were limited. So there was a very spare sort of interest what's going on both in social and political world, left-wing politics, and Zionism at the same time developing. So I was too young to take part in it still. But it was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. You said that there was-- that the Poles were quite anti-Semitic. Did you experience, personally, any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Ukrainians were even worse. Well, it was part of life. You just accepted it, you know, that this is part of that. I was never invited to-- to a Polish home or my friends. We met in school. And, of course, the test came during the war, which you just realize how deeply rooted. This was very painful to accept, that people you went to school and you play in the foreground, you know, enjoyed the-- the-- the-- the-- the situation, gloated almost, on what was happening to us. You had very little-- but I must say, there were exception. We mustn't have it one sided. There were exception, people who did help, who came out. There were people, and in my experience, I did have people, who, without any material benefits, helped me along in my survival.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, also, we have very sad experiences. I do want to emphasize that there were people, a very small-- unfortunately, a very small percentage. And in a way, Poland has a very bad record of-- of-- of-- in history, because all the extermination camps were there, because the local population collaborated almost and helped along. But there is-- there is-- we mustn't forget that there were people who did help, and the people who survived had at one stage or another, you know, perhaps have some help from people who did help. When did you first become aware of the, sort of, acute difficulties and the beginning of the, sort of, troubles?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean of the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war started. Well, you know, I remember the first day of war, September-- beginning of September. My friend came running to me, she said, isn't it marvelous? History will write about us. You know, she-- poor little thing, she didn't realize what is coming. We didn't realize, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the war broke out, the war lasted very quickly, you know. Poland fell very quickly. And we had the Germans coming in the second half of September. And they were staying about two weeks, and they retreated because, you know, there was the pact between the Poles-- and the both Germans and the Russians, and they retreated. So in this two weeks, we had-- we had the German army coming. And they were very well equipped, very tall, very Aryan looking. Very strong. What do you remember of their arrival?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember-- I remember they were stationed in our house, still being the big house, so they need to-- required accommodation. And they had wonderful equipment for shaving and, you know, being a child, I used to look on. And there was one of them, who came to my mother and said, you know, you had your life, but I'm sorry for your child. Well, we didn't understand what was coming, you know. But he said, I'm sorry for your daughter. And it was very short. We started-- they required people to go to work, men only. So they would just take off people. And by going away, after two weeks, they just took 18 people and shot them. Just the Jewish people, as a goodbye present. You know, this was a shock, you can imagine, at that time. It sort of broke down. There were three-- I remember three sons from one family who were shot. They were just going as normal to work, because they were required to, forced labor. And-- and they were just shot in the morning. The next day they moved away. And so when the Russian came, it was almost like a liberation in a way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these things discussed? Were you able to discuss things with your family? Did your parents discuss--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. You know, this was the beginning, and the war was going on, and we didn't know what was coming on. And the Russians came in. We were all heartbroken at that time. We had time to lament, to sit down, and to-- The first impact, you know, was very strong. Later on, you didn't have time even to lament. But at that time, you know, it was a shock to the family. I remember, my father died at that time. Because as the town was bombarded, although it was a little town, there was a factory and it was bombarded. We moved to the villages. And my grandfather had diabetes, and my father used to give him injection. And in this upheaval, the needle was broken, and he couldn't find, and there was no other neither doctor or chemist to get to another. So he really died of poisoning-- sugar poisoning-- because he didn't have the insulin at that time. And this is also a major tragedy at that time. We didn't realize what was coming. But one learned in time that this was really, relatively, nothing to what was coming to going to come. But we didn't know what was coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you could have-- you could have no idea of that, either.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not only that, we were also in the position, we didn't have a choice. You see, whereas before the war, some people from Austria, from Germany, not all, but some would escape. Some people-- but in our situation, in 1939-- end of 1939, the German-- the Russians came in. We had very little choice at that time what to do. So we were sitting, but then the Russian came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
